title: Salads and Dressings
content:
SALAD PRINCIPLES

Blend of flavors: Often unusual combinations (oranges with pickled onions, etc.)
Contrast of textures: Soft with fibrous/crisp (fish with pickles and celery)
Contrast of color: Vivid foods (carrots, beets, peppers) with pale; or colorful topping
Arrangement: Neat, attractive placement on greens

FOUNDATION FOODS

Fresh fruits and vegetables: Wash; set in cool place to crisp. Slice, dice, cube, or shred for easy eating.

Canned/cooked: Should have crispness; drain well. Prepare as fresh.

Dried fruits: May be used cooked or uncooked; whole, halved, diced, or shredded.

Cooked meat/chicken: Dice or shred when freed from bone and gristle.

Fish: Flake or shred.

Greens: Lettuce, watercress, cabbage, endive, spinach, romaine.

CARRIERS OF BULK AND FLAVOR

Cereals: Macaroni, rice, spaghetti
Cheese, nuts, prepared cereals, candies
Hard-cooked eggs

COMBINING

Thoroughly chill all prepared ingredients before combining. If marinating: toss with well-seasoned French dressing; stand 1 hour before serving.

Mix by tossing lightly with two forks. Pile loosely on crisp greens. Top with dressing.

Or: Mix with mayonnaise or boiled dressing immediately before serving.

Gelatin Salads: Fruits, vegetables, meats, chicken, fish in well-blended combinations added to gelatin base. Provides solidity and structure.

Frozen Salads: Foods blended with creamy salad dressing. Freeze in tray or mold packed in ice and salt (4:1) until dressing solidified (about 4 hours).

DRESSINGS

Three general types: French, Boiled, Mayonnaise. Interchangeable according to preference.

French Dressing variations: Add honey, fruit juice, grated cheese, seasonings
Boiled Dressing variations: Add whipped cream, whipped evaporated milk, chopped hard-cooked egg, relishes, pickles
Mayonnaise variations: Same as boiled; forms base for Thousand Island and Russian

Prepared Commercial Dressings: French, boiled, mayonnaise types available. Acceptable when time short; lend themselves to variations.
